explicit_text: I was born in Wagner, South Dakota, and lived there until 1942 when I was 17 years old. Wagner is where my roots are, and it’s in the southeast corner of South Dakota, near where my great-grandfather, Chief Running Bull of the Yankton Sioux tribe, lived. Leaving Wagner was a big step for me as I went to work in a defense plant during the Great Depression before eventually joining the Marines.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, I worked at a defense plant to make some money before joining the Marine Corps. It was a tough time for many people, and I stayed at that job until 1948. The Depression shaped a lot of my early life and influenced my decision to eventually enlist in the military.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Marine Corps in 1948 after working through the Depression years. My training was at Camp Elliott in San Diego, California, where I spent about four to five months preparing as an infantryman and machine gunner. I served with the Fourth Marine Raider Battalion and later with Chesty Puller's A Company, 1st Battalion, 7th Marines, staying with that unit throughout my service.

My training was at a camp in San Diego, California, where I spent about four to five months preparing as an infantryman and machine gunner. I served with an elite raider battalion and later with the company led by the legendary officer Chesty Puller, staying with that unit throughout my service.

explicit_text: My great-grandfather was Chief Running Bull of the Yankton Sioux tribe. He passed away in 1900, long before I was born, but his legacy was a significant part of my family history. Being connected to Chief Running Bull gave me a strong sense of identity and pride in my Sioux heritage.

explicit_text: I am descended from the Yankton Sioux tribe, specifically through my great-grandfather, Chief Running Bull. Though I grew up outside the reservation, my connection to the Yankton Sioux shaped my understanding of who I am.

explicit_text: I went to boot camp at Camp Elliott in San Diego, California, on the West Coast. I spent about four to five months there training as an infantryman and machine gunner before shipping out in March 1944. Camp Elliott was where I learned the skills that would carry me through the rest of my Marine Corps service.

explicit_text: San Diego, California, was where I trained at Camp Elliott and later shipped out to the Pacific. After leaving Omaha, Nebraska, I took a Pullman train to San Diego before boarding the USS Polk to head overseas. San Diego was a key staging point for many of us Marines during the war.

explicit_text: When I first went overseas in March 1944, I joined the Fourth Marine Raider Battalion, although they had officially been disbanded in February of that year. Despite that, I was assigned to them initially before being transferred to Chesty Puller's A Company. The Raider Battalion was a prestigious unit, and I was proud to be part of it, even briefly.

explicit_text: Chesty Puller was the legendary Marine officer in command of A Company, 1st Battalion, 7th Marines, the unit I served with after the Raider Battalion was disbanded. I joined his company in Omaha, Nebraska, and stayed with them throughout my Marine Corps career. Serving under Puller was an honor, and his leadership was something we all respected deeply.

We saw intense combat, including the Battle of Peleliu, and I was proud to be part of such a dedicated group.

explicit_text: I was transported overseas on the USS Polk, a troop carrier that took me from San Diego to the Pacific islands. The ship was our home for the journey to New Caledonia, where I first arrived in the combat zone. The USS Polk played a crucial role in moving troops like me to the front lines during the war.

explicit_text: I shared a blanket with Bill Veeck, a fellow Marine, while we slept under a Higgins boat during our deployment. Bill Veeck lost a leg, and we had a close bond during those tough times. Sharing that blanket was a small comfort amid the chaos of war.

explicit_text: My first stop overseas was New Caledonia after leaving San Diego aboard the USS Polk. From there, we moved on to the Russell Islands and then Pavuvu as part of our Pacific campaign. New Caledonia was an important staging area before heading into combat zones.

explicit_text: After New Caledonia, we moved to the Russell Islands as part of our progression toward the front lines. The Russell Islands were one of the stepping stones in the Pacific before we reached Pavuvu and eventually Peleliu. It was part of the chain of islands we secured during the war.

explicit_text: Pavuvu was another island we were stationed on after the Russell Islands, continuing our advance through the Pacific. It was part of the preparation and buildup before the intense fighting at Peleliu. Pavuvu gave us a place to rest and regroup before heading into battle.

explicit_text: The Battle of Peleliu was my first combat experience, landing on September 15th, 1944, after months of training and travel. We made an opposed landing under heavy enemy fire, with explosions and machine gun rounds hitting around us. The beach was covered with dead Marines, and I spent the night alone in a swamp surrounded by Japanese soldiers. It was a brutal battle that lasted about fifty days, and I was wounded three days after landing.
